learning_objectives: Define business process management and its lifecycle
Distinguish between functional and process-oriented organizations
Identify the role of BPM in organizational performance improvement
readings: Dumas et al., Fundamentals of Business Process Management, Ch. 1-2
Hammer & Champy, Reengineering the Corporation, Ch. 1

lecture_notes.introduction: Business processes are the way organizations deliver value. Managing processes systematically improves efficiency, quality, and customer satisfaction.
key_concepts: Business process: a set of activities that transform inputs into outputs of value to a customer
BPM lifecycle: design, model, execute, monitor, optimize
Functional silos vs. process orientation: departments optimize locally; processes optimize end-to-end
Process owner: the person accountable for the end-to-end performance of a process
Process maturity: ad hoc, defined, measured, managed, optimized
lecture_notes.demonstration: Mapping a simple order-to-cash process and identifying functional handoff points where delays occur
setup_requirements: Process mapping tools (Visio, Lucidchart, or equivalent)

review.value: Each department optimizes for its own KPIs; the customer experience suffers at handoff points between departments
Managing end-to-end processes focuses optimization on what the customer experiences, not what each department measures

learning_objectives: Create process models using Business Process Model and Notation (BPMN 2.0)
Apply BPMN elements: events, activities, gateways, flows, pools, and lanes
Model complex process logic including parallel and conditional paths

lecture_notes.introduction: BPMN is the international standard for process modeling. It provides a visual language that both business and technical stakeholders can understand.
key_concepts: BPMN elements: start/end events, tasks, subprocesses, exclusive/parallel/inclusive gateways
Sequence flows and message flows: how activities connect and how processes communicate
Pools and lanes: representing organizations and roles within a process
Error handling: boundary events, error events, compensation
Best practices: naming conventions, granularity levels, documentation
lecture_notes.demonstration: Modeling a procurement process in BPMN with parallel approval paths and exception handling
setup_requirements: BPMN modeling tool (Signavio, Bizagi Modeler, or draw.io with BPMN library)

review.value: Exclusive (XOR): one path; Parallel (AND): all paths; Inclusive (OR): one or more paths; each has distinct BPMN symbols
High-level models show 5-10 steps for executive communication; detailed models show every step for implementation

learning_objectives: Analyze process performance using cycle time, throughput, and resource utilization
Apply value stream analysis to identify waste
Calculate process efficiency and identify bottlenecks

lecture_notes.introduction: Process analysis quantifies how a process performs and identifies where improvements will have the greatest impact.
key_concepts: Cycle time: total time from process start to process end (including wait time)
Processing time vs. wait time: the ratio reveals process efficiency
Throughput: number of process instances completed per time period
Little's Law: WIP = Throughput x Cycle Time; relates the three flow metrics
Bottleneck identification: the activity with the longest cycle time limits overall throughput
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyzing a customer onboarding process with timing data to identify the bottleneck and calculate efficiency

review.value: If you know any two of WIP, throughput, and cycle time, you can calculate the third; fundamental for flow optimization
Processing time / Cycle time; most processes are 5-25% efficient, meaning 75-95% of time is waiting

learning_objectives: Apply process redesign patterns to improve performance
Use Six Sigma DMAIC methodology for process improvement
Balance efficiency, quality, and flexibility in process design
readings: Pyzdek, The Six Sigma Handbook, Ch. 4-6

lecture_notes.introduction: After analysis comes redesign. Systematic improvement methods ensure changes are effective and sustainable.
key_concepts: Redesign patterns: task elimination, task automation, parallelization, resequencing, specialist-generalist trade-off
DMAIC: Define (problem statement), Measure (current performance), Analyze (root causes), Improve (design solution), Control (sustain gains)
Quick wins vs. structural changes: implement easy improvements first while planning larger redesigns
Process simulation: modeling redesigned processes before implementation to predict outcomes
Change management for process change: people must adopt new processes for improvements to stick
lecture_notes.demonstration: Applying three redesign patterns to an insurance claims process and estimating the impact on cycle time

review.value: The most powerful improvement; if a task adds no value to the customer, remove it entirely
Structured methodology prevents jumping to solutions before understanding the problem; measure before you improve

learning_objectives: Understand RPA, workflow automation, and AI-assisted process automation
Design processes that leverage technology effectively

lecture_notes.introduction: Technology transforms processes. But automating a bad process just produces bad results faster. Fix the process first, then automate.
key_concepts: Automation spectrum: rule-based RPA, workflow engines, AI-assisted decision making, fully autonomous
RPA (Robotic Process Automation): software bots performing repetitive, rule-based tasks
Automation suitability criteria: high volume, rule-based, stable, digital inputs/outputs
Process-first: redesign the process before automating; do not automate waste
Human-in-the-loop: keeping human judgment for exceptions and complex decisions
lecture_notes.demonstration: Evaluating five candidate processes for RPA using a suitability assessment matrix

review.value: Fix the process first (eliminate waste, simplify steps), then automate what remains; automating a broken process magnifies the brokenness
Best for high-volume, rule-based, repetitive tasks with digital inputs; poor for tasks requiring judgment, creativity, or unstructured data

learning_objectives: Design a process governance framework for an organization
Build a culture of continuous process improvement

lecture_notes.introduction: Sustainable BPM requires governance: clear ownership, monitoring, and a culture that treats process improvement as ongoing, not a one-time project.
key_concepts: Process governance: ownership, standards, monitoring, improvement authority
Process monitoring dashboards: real-time KPIs for cycle time, quality, throughput, cost
Continuous improvement culture: Kaizen mindset, employee-driven improvements, recognition
Process repository: central documentation of all organizational processes
BPM maturity: moving from project-based improvement to embedded process management
lecture_notes.demonstration: Designing a process governance framework and monitoring dashboard for a mid-size organization

review.value: Every process needs an owner with authority and accountability for its end-to-end performance; without ownership, nobody is responsible
Continuous small improvements driven by the people who do the work; more sustainable than periodic large-scale reengineering

questions.question: A process has 5 sequential activities with the following processing times: A (5 min), B (10 min), C (3 min), D (15 min), E (7 min). The total cycle time (including wait time between activities) is 120 minutes. Calculate the process efficiency and identify the bottleneck.
questions.answer: Total processing time = 5 + 10 + 3 + 15 + 7 = 40 minutes. Process efficiency = Processing time / Cycle time = 40/120 = 33.3%. The bottleneck is Activity D at 15 minutes (longest processing time), which limits the maximum throughput to 60/15 = 4 instances per hour.

rubric.key: Correctly identifies bottleneck with throughput impact

questions.question: Explain the difference between an exclusive gateway (XOR) and a parallel gateway (AND) in BPMN, and give one example of when each would be used in a real business process.
questions.answer: Exclusive gateway (XOR): exactly one outgoing path is taken based on a condition. Example: after a credit check, the process either proceeds to 'approve loan' OR 'reject loan' based on the credit score. Only one path executes. Parallel gateway (AND): all outgoing paths are taken simultaneously. Example: after receiving an order, the process simultaneously starts 'pick items from warehouse,' 'generate invoice,' and 'notify shipping department.' All three activities happen in parallel. A corresponding parallel gateway later synchronizes the paths before proceeding to 'ship order.'

questions.question: Explain why 'automating a bad process just produces bad results faster' and describe the correct sequence of steps when technology is proposed to solve a process problem.
questions.answer: If a process has unnecessary steps, handoff delays, rework loops, or other waste, automating it speeds up the waste rather than eliminating it. For example, automating a purchase order approval process that has 7 unnecessary approval steps means emails are sent faster, but the fundamental problem (too many approvals) remains. Correct sequence: (1) Map the current process (as-is), (2) Analyze to identify waste, bottlenecks, and root causes, (3) Redesign the process to eliminate waste and simplify (to-be), (4) Validate the redesigned process works manually or in pilot, (5) THEN automate the improved process. This is sometimes called 'pave the cowpath' error: automating the path cows happen to take rather than designing a proper road.

rubric.key: Explains why automation without improvement fails

questions.question: Using Little's Law, a process has an average of 60 items in progress (WIP) and completes 20 items per day (throughput). What is the average cycle time? If you reduce WIP to 30 while maintaining throughput, what happens to cycle time?
questions.answer: Little's Law: WIP = Throughput x Cycle Time, so Cycle Time = WIP / Throughput. Current: Cycle Time = 60 / 20 = 3 days. After WIP reduction: Cycle Time = 30 / 20 = 1.5 days. Reducing WIP by 50% cuts cycle time by 50%. This is the fundamental principle behind Kanban WIP limits: reducing work in progress directly reduces how long each item takes to complete.

major_assignments.title: Business Process Improvement Project
major_assignments.description: Select a real business process, model it in BPMN, analyze its performance, redesign it for improvement, and propose an implementation plan.
objectives: Create an as-is BPMN process model with accurate detail
Analyze the process for waste, bottlenecks, and inefficiencies
Design a to-be process using redesign patterns and evaluate automation opportunities
Propose an implementation plan with expected performance improvements
deliverables: As-is BPMN process model with process narrative (3-4 pages)
Process analysis with performance metrics, waste identification, and bottleneck analysis (3-4 pages)
To-be BPMN process model with redesign rationale (3-4 pages)
Automation assessment for the redesigned process (1-2 pages)
Implementation plan with expected performance improvements and success metrics (2-3 pages)

criteria: BPMN notation used correctly with proper symbols
Process model captures the actual current process accurately
Narrative describes the process clearly with roles and systems identified
Redesign patterns applied correctly and creatively
To-be model is valid BPMN and logically sound
Implementation plan is realistic with change management considerations

weekly_quizzes.description: Quizzes on BPMN modeling, process analysis, and redesign

midterm_examination.description: BPMN modeling, process analysis, and Little's Law

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam covering all BPM topics including automation and governance

class_participation.description: Participation in process modeling exercises, analysis workshops, and case studies

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is expected for all sessions. BPMN modeling workshops require in-person participation.
course_policies.late_submissions: Assignments lose 10% per day late. No submissions accepted more than 5 days past the due date.
course_policies.collaboration: Process modeling exercises may be done in pairs. The BPM project is individual work.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All process models and analyses must be original work. Using AI to generate BPMN diagrams without understanding the notation defeats the learning objective.
course_policies.technology_requirements: BPMN modeling tool (free options: Bizagi Modeler, draw.io). Spreadsheet for process analysis calculations.
